content:
9 min read
1 hour ago

--

Building a website is easy.
Building one that survives Tatkal booking is engineering.
Recently, I came across updates around IRCTC’s new booking platform and upgraded Passenger Reservation System. Reports mention that the new system is expected to handle over 1.5 lakh ticket bookings per minute, compared to around 32,000 bookings per minute earlier.
That number is not just a product update.
That number is a system design problem wearing a railway uniform.
Because when lakhs of users open the app at the same time, search for trains, check availability, fill passenger details, apply quota rules, make payments, and expect confirmation instantly, the backend is not just serving pages.
It is fighting a traffic storm.
And the interesting question is: If I had to design an IRCTC-like ticket booking system from scratch, how would I build it?
Let’s break it down.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Real Problem Is Not Booking Tickets
At first, booking a train ticket sounds simple.
A user searches for a train, selects a seat, pays, and gets a ticket.
Cute.
But on an Indian scale, this simple flow becomes chaos.
The system must handle:

• Massive traffic spikes

• Millions of concurrent users

• Seat inventory conflicts

• Payment gateway failures

• Retry storms

• Bot attacks

• Queue management

• Real-time availability updates

• Fairness during Tatkal windows

• Zero or near-zero downtime
The hardest systems are not the ones with the most features.
They are the ones that keep working when an entire country clicks Book Now at the same second.

Why Tatkal Traffic Is Different
Most products have gradual traffic.
E-commerce traffic grows during sales. Food apps spike during lunch. Streaming apps spike during big matches.
But Tatkal is different.
Tatkal is a scheduled stampede.
At 10:00 AM or 11:00 AM, demand does not slowly increase. It explodes.
That means the system must be ready before the spike happens.
You cannot auto-scale after the fire starts and hope the building forgives you.
The system needs:
Pre-warmed servers
Precomputed train data
Aggressive caching
Queue-based booking flow
Rate limiting
Bot protection
Payment resilience
Database write protectionIf one layer fails, users do not say: “Ah, yes, distributed systems are hard.”
They say, “IRCTC is down again.”
And honestly, fair.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Tatkal Traffic Spike
Product Principle: Fairness Matters More Than Speed Alone
For a ticket booking system, speed is important.
But fairness is more important.
If the system is only optimised for speed, bots and high-speed agents win.
A good system should optimise for:

• Genuine users

• Transparent waiting

• No duplicate seat allocation

• Stable payment flow

• Clear failure states

• Fair retry behaviour
The product question is not just: How fast can we book tickets?
It is: How do we make sure the right users get a fair chance without the system collapsing?
That is where product management and system design meet.

High-Level Architecture
At a high level, I would design the system like this:
User App / Website
↓
CDN + Edge Layer
↓
API Gateway
↓
Auth + Bot Protection
↓
Search Service
↓
Availability Service
↓
Booking Orchestrator
↓
Seat Locking Service
↓
Payment Service
↓
Ticket Confirmation Service
↓
Notification Service
↓
Databases + Cache + Queue + Event StreamEach service should do one job well.
The search service should not lock seats.
The payment service should not calculate train availability.
The booking service should not send emails.
Separation matters because during peak load, you need to scale bottlenecks independently.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

High-Level Architecture

Step 1: CDN and Edge Caching
Not every request should hit the backend.
Static assets, homepage data, station lists, train metadata, banners, help pages, and common route data can be served from CDN or edge cache.
This reduces unnecessary load.
Example:
Static assets → CDN
Station search suggestions → Edge cache
Train route metadata → Cache
User-specific booking flow → BackendThe rule is simple:
If the data does not change every second, don’t ask the database every second.
Databases are not emotional support systems. Stop calling them for everything.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

CDN + Edge Layer

Step 2: Search Should Be Fast, Cached, and Read-Optimised
Searching trains is read-heavy.
Millions of users may search the same popular routes:

• Delhi to Mumbai

• Lucknow to Delhi

• Patna to Delhi

• Kolkata to Bangalore

• Chennai to Hyderabad
Instead of hitting the core database repeatedly, the system should use:

• Search indexes

• Redis cache

• Precomputed route results

• Read replicas

• Availability snapshots
The search result does not need to lock seats.
It only needs to show possible options quickly.
Booking begins only after the user selects a train and quota.
This separation protects the core reservation system from unnecessary pressure.

Step 3: Availability Is Not the Same as Booking
This is very important.
Availability shown to users is a snapshot.
Booking is a transaction.
If 20 seats are visible at 10:00:01 AM, it does not mean all users can claim them.
So I would separate:
Availability Service → fast read model
Seat Locking Service → strict write model
Booking Confirmation → final transactionThe availability service can be eventually consistent.
The seat locking service cannot.
Because two users cannot get the same seat.
Unless you want the final train coach to become a distributed systems meetup.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Availability vs Booking

Step 4: Seat Locking
Seat locking is the heart of the system.
When a user clicks Book Now, the system should not immediately confirm the ticket.
It should create a temporary lock.
Example:
User selects train
↓
System checks quota and availability
↓
Seat lock created for 3-5 minutes
↓
User completes passenger details and payment
↓
Payment success
↓
Ticket confirmedThe lock should have an expiry.
If payment fails or user drops off, the seat returns to inventory.
This avoids both overbooking and permanent seat blocking.
Seat lock table:
seat_locks
- lock_id
- train_id
- journey_date
- quota
- class
- user_id
- status
- expires_atPossible statuses:
LOCKED
CONFIRMED
EXPIRED
CANCELLED
PAYMENT_FAILEDPress enter or click to view image in full size

Seat Lock Lifecycle
Step 5: Queue the Booking Requests
During Tatkal, everyone should not directly hit the booking database.
That is how systems die dramatically.
Instead, booking requests should go through a queue.
Book Now click
↓
Request validation
↓
Queue token generated
↓
Booking request enters queue
↓
Workers process requests at controlled speed
↓
Seat lock result returnedThis gives the system control.
If the database can safely process 50,000 booking attempts per minute, don’t let 5 million requests punch it in the face.
The queue protects the core system.
It also gives users a better experience:
You are in queue.
Estimated wait: 38 seconds.
Do not refresh.
Refreshing should not improve queue position.
Otherwise, the system accidentally trains users to attack it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Queue Management
Step 6: Rate Limiting and Bot Protection
High-demand ticketing systems attract bots.
So the system needs multiple layers of protection:

• Login-based rate limits

• Device fingerprinting

• IP throttling

• Aadhaar/KYC-linked verification for sensitive flows

• CAPTCHA or invisible bot detection

• Agent restrictions during peak windows

• Suspicious behavior scoring
A normal user might click Book Now once or twice.
A bot may hit the endpoint hundreds of times.
The system should detect that before it reaches the booking engine.
Rate limiting should happen at:
Edge layer
API gateway
User account level
Device level
Booking endpoint level
Payment retry levelSecurity is not one wall.
It is multiple doors asking, “Bro, why are you running?”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Rate Limiting Layers
Step 7: Payment System Must Be Asynchronous and Idempotent
Payments are messy.
A user may pay successfully, but the response may time out.
The bank may debit the amount, but the callback may arrive late.
The payment gateway may fail even when the booking system is fine.
So the payment flow must be designed carefully.
Important rules:
Every payment request should have an idempotency key.
Payment success should be confirmed through webhook/callback.
Ticket confirmation should happen only once.
Failed payments should release seat locks.
Refund flow should be automated.Idempotency means that if the user retries, the system does not create duplicate bookings.
Example:
idempotency_key = user_id + train_id + journey_date + lock_idThis prevents duplicate ticket creation during retry storms.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Payment Flow

Step 8: Avoid Overselling With Optimistic Locking
Seat inventory is a concurrency problem.
If many users attempt to book the same train at the same time, the system must avoid overselling.
One way is optimistic locking.
Example:
UPDATE seat_inventory
SET available_seats = available_seats - 1,
version = version + 1
WHERE train_run_id = ?
AND class = ?
AND quota = ?
AND available_seats > 0
AND version = ?;If the update succeeds, the seat is locked.
If it fails, another request got there first.
This keeps inventory correct without locking the entire database table forever.

Step 9: Retry Storms Are Dangerous
When systems slow down, users retry.
When users retry, systems slow down more.
That is called a retry storm.
A bad client does this:
Request failed.
Retry immediately.
Retry again.
Retry again.
Refresh page.
Open another tab.
Try from mobile.
Try from laptop.
Panic.A good system does this:
Request failed.
Retry after 2 seconds.
Then 4 seconds.
Then 8 seconds.
Show clear status.
Prevent duplicate booking.The frontend should also help protect the backend.
Disable repeated Book Now clicks.
Show queue state.
Show payment status clearly.
Do not make users guess.
Guessing creates retries.
Retries create load.
Load creates sadness.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Retry Storm

Step 10: Observability
At this scale, monitoring is not optional.
The system needs real-time visibility into:

• Requests per second

• Queue length

• Booking success rate

• Payment failure rate

• Seat lock expiry rate

• Database latency

• Cache hit ratio

• Error rate

• Bot traffic

• Regional traffic spikes
Good dashboards should answer:
Is the system slow?
Where is it slow?
Is booking failing?
Is payment failing?
Is database overloaded?
Are bots attacking?
Are users stuck in queue?If you cannot see the system, you cannot operate the system.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Observability Dashboard
Step 11: Graceful Degradation
During peak traffic, not every feature deserves equal priority.
Booking must work.
Everything else is secondary.
If the load becomes too high, the system can temporarily disable:

• Recommendations

• Ads

• Heavy images

• Non-critical animations

• Personalization

• Low-priority analytics

• Fancy UI experiments
A ticket booking system should have a “survival mode.”
In survival mode, the product says:
Keep login working.
Keep search working.
Keep booking working.
Keep payment working.
Pause everything else.Because nobody cares about a beautiful carousel when the ticket is gone.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Graceful Degradation
Final Architecture
The final system would look like this:

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Final Architecture Map
This is not just about adding servers.
It is about controlling flow.
The best high-scale systems do not allow traffic to hit everything directly.
They shape traffic.
They queue traffic.
They cache repeated reads.
They protect critical writes.
They degrade gracefully.
That is the differen
